routes.label: Abraham's Journey
routes.description: Abraham's journey from Ur of the Chaldeans to Canaan (c. 2000 BC), sojourning in Haran, then to Shechem, Egypt, and settling at Hebron (Gen 12–22).

routes.label: Jacob's Journey
routes.description: Jacob flees to Haran to serve Laban, returns with his family, wrestles with God at Peniel, and is reunited with Isaac at Hebron before moving to Egypt (Gen 28–46).

routes.label: The Exodus
routes.description: The Exodus from Egypt — Moses leads Israel out of Goshen, through the wilderness of Sinai to the base of Sinai/Horeb, and then north toward Kadesh Barnea before the forty-year wilderness wandering (Ex 12–Num 14).

routes.label: Southern Campaign
routes.description: Joshua's southern campaign — crossing the Jordan at Gilgal, taking Jericho and Ai, then defeating the Amorite coalition at Gibeon and pressing south through the Shephelah (Josh 6–10).

routes.label: Northern Campaign
routes.description: Joshua's northern campaign — marching from Gilgal to defeat the northern coalition led by Jabin of Hazor at the Waters of Merom; Hazor burned (Josh 11).

routes.label: Assyrian Campaigns
routes.description: Assyrian campaigns — Tiglath-Pileser III, Shalmaneser V, and Sennacherib march west against Israel and Judah (734–701 BC), destroying Samaria in 722 BC and besieging Jerusalem in 701 BC (2 Kgs 15–19).

routes.label: Babylonian Campaigns
routes.description: Babylonian campaigns — Nebuchadnezzar II marches against Jerusalem in three waves (605, 597, 586 BC), destroying the Temple and deporting the population in the final siege (2 Kgs 24–25).

routes.label: Return from Exile
routes.description: Return from Babylonian exile — Zerubbabel leads the first return of 42,360 exiles (538 BC); Ezra leads a second wave in 458 BC; Nehemiah arrives in 445 BC to rebuild the walls (Ezra 1–2; Neh 2).

routes.label: Alexander's Campaign
routes.description: Alexander the Great's eastern campaign (334–323 BC) — from Macedonia through Asia Minor, defeating Persia at Issus and Gaugamela, conquering Egypt, Babylon, and reaching as far as the Punjab.

routes.label: Paul — 1st Journey
routes.description: Paul's First Missionary Journey (c. AD 46–48) — from Antioch to Cyprus, then through Pamphylia, Antioch of Pisidia, Iconium, Lystra, and Derbe before retracing and returning to Antioch (Acts 13–14).

routes.label: Paul — 2nd Journey
routes.description: Paul's Second Missionary Journey (c. AD 49–52) — revisiting Lystra (Timothy joins), crossing to Macedonia at Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea, Athens, Corinth (18 months), and returning via Ephesus and Caesarea (Acts 15:36–18:22).

routes.label: Paul — 3rd Journey
routes.description: Paul's Third Missionary Journey (c. AD 53–58) — three years at Ephesus ('all Asia heard the word'), then through Macedonia and Greece, back via Troas, Miletus, Caesarea, and Jerusalem (Acts 18:23–21:17).

routes.label: Paul's Voyage to Rome
routes.description: Paul's Voyage to Rome (c. AD 59–60) — under guard from Caesarea, shipwrecked at Malta ('not one life will be lost'), wintering there, and arriving in Rome to preach 'with all boldness and without hindrance' (Acts 27–28).

routes.label: Joseph Sold into Egypt
routes.description: Joseph's brothers sell him to Ishmaelite traders at Dothan; he is taken to Egypt and eventually rises to second-in-command under Pharaoh (Gen 37–41).

routes.label: Deborah's Campaign
routes.description: Deborah and Barak's campaign against Sisera — Israel's army descends from Mount Tabor; the Canaanite chariot force is routed in the Jezreel Valley at the River Kishon (Judg 4–5).

routes.label: Ruth's Journey
routes.description: Naomi and Ruth journey from Moab back to Bethlehem — 'Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay' (Ruth 1).

routes.label: Jonah's Sea Flight
routes.description: Jonah boards a ship at Joppa to flee to Tarshish; a great storm arises and he is thrown overboard, swallowed by a great fish for three days (Jon 1).

routes.label: Jonah to Nineveh
routes.description: Jonah travels from the sea to Nineveh, proclaims judgment, and the city repents — 'Should I not pity Nineveh?' (Jon 3–4).

routes.label: Jeremiah Taken to Egypt
routes.description: After Jerusalem's fall, a remnant of Judah forcibly takes Jeremiah to Egypt against his warnings, settling at Tahpanhes — where he prophesied the Babylonian invasion of Egypt (Jer 42–44).

routes.label: Peter's Journeys
routes.description: Peter travels from Jerusalem to Samaria, then to Lydda, Joppa (raising Tabitha), and Caesarea (Cornelius, Gentile Pentecost), showing the gospel's expansion from Jerusalem (Acts 8–10).

routes.label: Philip's Journey
routes.description: Philip the Evangelist preaches in Samaria, baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch on the road from Jerusalem to Gaza, then is carried by the Spirit to Azotus and up the coast to Caesarea (Acts 8).

routes.label: John's Journey
routes.description: John's journeys — from Jerusalem with Peter to Samaria (Acts 8), traditional ministry in Ephesus and Asia Minor, exile to Patmos under Domitian where he received the Revelation (Rev 1:9).

positions.note: In Ur of the Chaldeans — called by God (Gen 12:1)
Stopping in Haran — 'he went out, not knowing where' (Heb 11:8)
At Shechem — first altar in the promised land (Gen 12:7)
In Egypt during famine (Gen 12:10)
In Hebron — settled by the oaks of Mamre (Gen 13:18)
Binding of Isaac on Moriah — 'God will provide' (Gen 22:8)
In Beersheba — 'well of the oath' (Gen 21:31)
Born at Beersheba — 'the son of the promise' (Gen 21:3)
At Mamre/Hebron — Abraham's household (Gen 22–23)
In Gerar — sojourns among the Philistines, digs wells (Gen 26:1)
Back at Beersheba — 'Do not be afraid; I am with you' (Gen 26:24)
At Hebron/Mamre — blesses Jacob instead of Esau (Gen 27)
Dies at Hebron aged 180 — Jacob and Esau bury him (Gen 35:29)
In Beersheba — fleeing Esau (Gen 28:10)
At Bethel — Jacob's ladder; 'Surely the LORD is in this place' (Gen 28:16)
In Haran — serving Laban for Rachel (Gen 29:20)
At Mahanaim — angels meet him (Gen 32:1)
At Peniel — wrestles with God; name changed to Israel (Gen 32:28)
Back in Hebron with his father Isaac (Gen 35:27)
Reunited with Joseph in Egypt (Gen 46:29)
In Hebron with Jacob — 'a coat of many colours' (Gen 37:3)
At Dothan — sold by his brothers for 20 pieces of silver (Gen 37:28)
In Memphis / Potiphar's house — 'the LORD was with Joseph' (Gen 39:2)
In Pharaoh's prison — interprets the cupbearer's dream (Gen 40)
Before Pharaoh — 'There is none so discerning and wise as you' (Gen 41:39)
In Goshen — brothers arrive for grain; weeps at the sight of Benjamin (Gen 45:14)
Reunites Jacob with the whole family — 'God sent me before you' (Gen 45:5)
Born in Egypt — hidden in the Nile (Ex 2:3)
Kills the Egyptian — flees from Pharaoh (Ex 2:15)
In Midian — forty years as shepherd (Ex 2:16)
The burning bush — 'I AM WHO I AM' (Ex 3:14)
Confronting Pharaoh — ten plagues (Ex 7–12)
At Sinai — receiving the Law (Ex 20)
On Mount Nebo — sees the promised land (Deut 34:1)
Led the fighting men out of Egypt on Passover night (Ex 12)
At Sinai — commander against Amalek; ascends with Moses (Ex 17:9)
At Kadesh Barnea — one of the two faithful spies (Num 14:6)
Base camp at Gilgal — first step into the land (Josh 4)
Victory at Gibeon — 'sun, stand still' (Josh 10:12)
Northern campaign — Hazor burns (Josh 11:11)
Dividing the land at Shiloh — 'not one word failed' (Josh 21:43)
At Kadesh Barnea — 'Let us go up at once' (Num 13:30)
Crosses the Jordan — base camp at Gilgal (Josh 4)
Receives Hebron at age 85 — 'as yet I am as strong' (Josh 14:11)
Conquers Debir; gives daughter Achsah the upper springs (Josh 15:16)
At her palm tree between Ramah and Bethel — 'she judged Israel' (Judg 4:4)
At Kedesh — summons Barak; 'Has not the LORD gone before you?' (Judg 4:14)
Mount Tabor — Sisera's 900 iron chariots routed at the Kishon (Judg 4:15)
At Ophrah — 'The LORD is with you, O mighty man of valor' (Judg 6:12)
At the Spring of Harod — 300 chosen by how they drink (Judg 7:7)
Rout of the Midianites — torches and trumpets (Judg 7:20)
East of the Jordan — captures Zebah and Zalmunna (Judg 8:12)
Returns to Ophrah — 'The LORD will rule over you' (Judg 8:23)
Born at Zorah — 'set apart to God from the womb' (Judg 13:5)
At Timnah — kills the lion; Philistine wife (Judg 14)
At Lehi — slays a thousand with a jawbone (Judg 15:15)
At Gaza — Delilah; 'The LORD has left me' (Judg 16:20)
Temple of Dagon, Gaza — 'Let me die with the Philistines' (Judg 16:30)
In Moab — 'Where you go I will go; your God shall be my God' (Ruth 1:16)
Arrives in Bethlehem at barley harvest (Ruth 1:22)
Threshing floor — kinsman-redeemer oath (Ruth 3:11)
At Shiloh — 'Speak, for your servant hears' (1 Sam 3:10)
At Ramah — Eli dies; ark captured; Samuel leads repentance
Circuit: Bethel — judging all Israel year by year (1 Sam 7:16)
Circuit: Gilgal — 'Has the LORD as great delight in burnt offerings?' (1 Sam 15:22)
Circuit: Mizpah — sets up Ebenezer; 'The LORD has helped us' (1 Sam 7:12)
Anoints Saul at Ramah (1 Sam 10:1)
At Bethlehem — anoints the boy David (1 Sam 16:7)
Dies at Ramah — all Israel mourns (1 Sam 25:1)
Anointed by Samuel at Ramah (1 Sam 9:2)
At Gibeah — his capital; 'the Spirit of God rushed upon him' (1 Sam 10:26)
At Jabesh-gilead — routs the Ammonites; confirmed as king (1 Sam 11:11)
At En-gedi — pursuing David; David spares his life (1 Sam 24:4)
At Endor — consults the medium; 'Tomorrow you shall be with me' (1 Sam 28:19)
Falls on Mount Gilboa — 'How the mighty have fallen' (2 Sam 1:19)
In Bethlehem — anointed as boy by Samuel (1 Sam 16:13)
Valley of Elah — 'You come with a sword; I come in the name of the LORD' (1 Sam 17:45)
Cave of Adullam — 400 men gather to him (1 Sam 22:2)
Wilderness of En-gedi — cuts Saul's robe in the cave (1 Sam 24:12)
At Ziklag — refuge among the Philistines (1 Sam 27:6)
Crowned king over Judah in Hebron (2 Sam 2:4)
Jerusalem — captures the city of David (2 Sam 5:7)
Receiving the Davidic covenant — 'I will establish his throne forever' (2 Sam 7:13)
Crowned in Jerusalem — 'Give your servant an understanding heart' (1 Kgs 3:9)
Temple dedicated — the cloud fills the house of the LORD (1 Kgs 8:10)
At Ezion-geber on the Red Sea — fleet to Ophir (1 Kgs 9:26)
Queen of Sheba visits — 'Your wisdom exceeds what I heard' (1 Kgs 10:7)
Heart turned away in old age — high places for foreign gods (1 Kgs 11:7)
At Ahab's court in Samaria — 'no rain except by my word' (1 Kgs 17:1)
Zarephath — sustained by the widow; her son raised (1 Kgs 17:9)
Mount Carmel — fire from heaven; 'The LORD, he is God' (1 Kgs 18:39)
Horeb — 'a still small voice'; 'What are you doing here, Elijah?' (1 Kgs 19:12)
Jezreel — Naboth's vineyard judgment (1 Kgs 21:17)
Taken up in a whirlwind at the Jordan — Elisha receives the mantle (2 Kgs 2:11)
At the Jordan — receives Elijah's mantle; 'Where is the God of Elijah?' (2 Kgs 2:14)
At Shunem — the Shunammite's son raised from the dead (2 Kgs 4:35)
Naaman healed in the Jordan — 'his flesh was restored like a child's' (2 Kgs 5:14)
At Dothan — 'Those with us are more than those with them' (2 Kgs 6:16)
At Damascus — anoints Hazael king; weeps over coming judgment (2 Kgs 8:11)
In Samaria — the siege lifted; 'a seah of flour for a shekel' (2 Kgs 7:1)
At Joppa — buys passage on a ship fleeing from the LORD (Jon 1:3)
In the Mediterranean — three days in the great fish (Jon 1:17)
At Nineveh — 'Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown' (Jon 3:4)
East of Nineveh — shade plant; 'Should I not pity Nineveh?' (Jon 4:11)
Call vision in Jerusalem — 'Here I am! Send me' (Isa 6:8)
To Ahaz — 'Behold, the virgin shall conceive' (Isa 7:14)
To Hezekiah — 185,000 Assyrians struck down (Isa 37:36)
Servant Songs — 'He was wounded for our transgressions' (Isa 53:5)
Called in Jerusalem — 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you' (Jer 1:5)
At the temple gate — 'Amend your ways'; the temple-sermon (Jer 7)
Imprisoned in Jerusalem — writes the letter to the exiles (Jer 29)
Jerusalem falls — Nebuchadnezzar releases him (Jer 40:4)
At Tahpanhes, Egypt — taken against his will (Jer 43:10)
In Jerusalem — priest, son of Buzi; about to be deported (Ezek 1:3)
Deported to Babylon with Jehoiachin (Ezek 1:1)
At Tel-abib on the Chebar River — 'I sat there overwhelmed' (Ezek 3:15)
Vision of Jerusalem's fall — 'The city has been struck' (Ezek 33:21)
The valley of dry bones — 'Can these bones live?' (Ezek 37:3)
Deported from Jerusalem as a youth (2 Kgs 24:1)
In Babylon — interpreting Nebuchadnezzar's dream (Dan 2:28)
The lion's den — 'my God sent his angel' (Dan 6:22)
At Susa — receiving the vision of the ram and goat (Dan 8)
In Susa — Mordecai's adopted daughter (Est 2:7)
Chosen as queen — 'for such a time as this' (Est 4:14)
Intercedes before the king — Haman hanged; Jews delivered (Est 7:10)
In Babylon — leads the first return of 42,360 exiles (Ezra 2:64)
In Jerusalem — lays the Temple foundation; people weep and shout (Ezra 3:12)
Temple completed — 'the glory of this latter temple shall be greater' (Hag 2:9)
Departs Babylon — 'a teacher well versed in the Law of Moses' (Ezra 7:6)
Arrives Jerusalem — reads the Law publicly; the people weep (Neh 8:9)
In Susa — cupbearer to Artaxerxes; hears of Jerusalem's broken walls (Neh 1:3)
Rebuilds Jerusalem's walls in 52 days — 'The God of heaven will make us prosper' (Neh 2:20)
In the Judean wilderness — 'The voice of one crying in the wilderness' (Isa 40:3)
At the Jordan — 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand' (Matt 3:2)
Baptizes Jesus — 'Behold, the Lamb of God' (John 1:29)
Imprisoned at Machaerus — 'Are you the one to come?' (Matt 11:3)
Beheaded at Machaerus — 'None greater than John' (Luke 7:28)
Born in Bethlehem — 'the Word became flesh' (John 1:14)
Boyhood in Nazareth — 'he grew in wisdom and stature' (Luke 2:52)
Baptized in the Jordan — 'This is my beloved Son' (Matt 3:17)
Forty days in the wilderness — tempted in every way (Matt 4:1)
Galilean ministry — Capernaum and the mount (Matt 4:17)
Triumphal entry into Jerusalem (Matt 21:9)
Crucified, buried, and risen — 'It is finished' (John 19:30)
In Jerusalem — Pentecost; 'Repent and be baptized' (Acts 2:38)
In Samaria — lays hands on new believers with John (Acts 8:14)
At Lydda — heals Aeneas; paralyzed eight years (Acts 9:34)
At Joppa — raises Tabitha from the dead (Acts 9:40)
At Caesarea — Cornelius; 'God shows no partiality' (Acts 10:34)
At Antioch — confronted by Paul over the gospel truth (Gal 2:14)
In Rome — martyred ~AD 64–68; 'Babylon' sends greetings (1 Pet 5:13)
In Jerusalem — one of the Seven; 'full of faith and the Holy Spirit' (Acts 6:5)
In Samaria — proclaims Christ; great joy in that city (Acts 8:8)
On the Gaza road — baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:38)
At Azotus/Ashdod — passes through preaching (Acts 8:40)
At Caesarea — 'Philip the evangelist'; hosts Paul (Acts 21:8)
In Jerusalem — Pentecost; the disciple Jesus loved (John 21:7)
In Samaria — confirms the Samaritan believers with Peter (Acts 8:14)
At Ephesus — letters to the seven churches written from here (Rev 1–3)
On Patmos — 'I was in the Spirit on the Lord's day' (Rev 1:9)
From Tarsus in Cilicia — 'a citizen of no ordinary city' (Acts 21:39)
In Jerusalem — persecuting the church; 'breathing threats and murder' (Acts 9:1)
Road to Damascus — blinding light; 'Saul, why do you persecute me?' (Acts 9:4)
In Arabia — post-conversion retreat; 'I did not consult with any human' (Gal 1:17)
At Antioch — sent out on the first missionary journey (Acts 13:3)
At Lystra — stoned and left for dead; rises and continues (Acts 14:19)
At Philippi — gospel enters Europe (Acts 16:14)
Three years at Ephesus — 'all Asia heard the word' (Acts 19:10)
Caesarea — imprisoned; appeals to Caesar (Acts 25:11)
Shipwrecked at Malta — 'not one of you will be lost' (Acts 27:44)
Rome — 'with all boldness and without hindrance' (Acts 28:31)

places.label: Ur
places.significance: Sumerian city-state on the Euphrates; birthplace of Abraham (Gen 11:31).

places.label: Haran
places.significance: Crossroads city on the trade route; Abraham stopped here before entering Canaan.

places.label: Shechem
places.significance: Abraham built the first altar here; later the city of Simeon and Levi's vengeance (Gen 34).

places.label: Bethel
places.significance: Jacob's ladder — 'Surely the LORD is in this place' (Gen 28:16).

places.label: Hebron
places.significance: Abraham's home in Canaan; Sarah, Abraham, Isaac, Rebekah, Jacob, and Leah buried here.

places.label: Beersheba
places.significance: 'Well of the oath' — boundary of the promised land; Abraham and Isaac made covenants here.

places.label: Mt. Moriah
places.significance: Where Abraham bound Isaac; later the site of Solomon's Temple and Calvary (2 Chr 3:1).

places.label: Dothan
places.significance: Joseph sold to Ishmaelite traders here (Gen 37:28); Elisha surrounded by Aramean army (2 Kgs 6:13).

places.label: Goshen
places.significance: The fertile Nile Delta region where Israel multiplied during the 430 years in Egypt.

places.label: Mt. Sinai
places.significance: God appeared to Moses in the burning bush; the Law given; the Tabernacle commanded (Ex 3, 20).

places.label: Kadesh Barnea
places.significance: Israel's long encampment during the 40 years of wilderness; the border of the promised land (Num 13).

places.label: Jericho
places.significance: First city taken in the conquest — walls fell at the shout of Israel (Josh 6:20).

places.label: Shiloh
places.significance: The Tabernacle rested here for ~300 years; Samuel grew up here; the ark was captured from here (1 Sam 4).

places.label: Gilgal
places.significance: First campsite in Canaan; memorial stones set up; later Samuel's circuit court (Josh 4:20).

places.label: Gibeon
places.significance: The Gibeonites' deception; Joshua's long day — 'Sun, stand still' (Josh 10:12).

places.label: Mt. Nebo
places.significance: Moses saw the entire promised land from here and died; God himself buried him (Deut 34:1–6).

places.label: Jerusalem
places.significance: David's capital; site of the Temple; crucifixion and resurrection of Christ.

places.label: Bethlehem
places.significance: City of David; birthplace of the Messiah (Mic 5:2; Luke 2:4).

places.label: Samaria
places.significance: Capital of the northern kingdom of Israel; fell to Assyria in 722 BC (2 Kgs 17:6).

places.label: Mt. Carmel
places.significance: Elijah defeated 450 prophets of Baal here — 'The LORD, he is God' (1 Kgs 18:39).

places.label: Valley of Elah
places.significance: David killed Goliath here — 'I come in the name of the LORD of hosts' (1 Sam 17:45).

places.label: Nineveh
places.significance: Assyrian capital; Jonah preached here; fell to Babylon in 612 BC.

places.label: Babylon
places.significance: Capital of the Babylonian Empire; Israel exiled here 586–538 BC (Ps 137:1).

places.label: Susa
places.significance: Persian winter capital; Daniel's visions; Esther and Mordecai; Nehemiah was cupbearer here.

places.label: Damascus
places.significance: Capital of Aram; Paul's conversion on the road here (Acts 9:3).

places.label: Nazareth
places.significance: Jesus' boyhood home — 'Can anything good come from Nazareth?' (John 1:46).

places.label: Capernaum
places.significance: Jesus' base for Galilean ministry; Peter's home; synagogue healings (Matt 4:13).

places.label: Jordan Ford
places.significance: Israel crossed here into Canaan; Jesus baptized here (Josh 3:17; Matt 3:13).

places.label: Antioch
places.significance: Believers first called 'Christians' here; base of Paul's three missionary journeys (Acts 11:26).

places.label: Ephesus
places.significance: Paul's three-year base; 'all Asia heard the word'; first of the seven churches (Acts 19:10).

places.label: Philippi
places.significance: First European church; Lydia and the jailer converted; Paul and Silas imprisoned (Acts 16:14).

places.label: Athens
places.significance: Paul preached on the Areopagus — 'In him we live and move and have our being' (Acts 17:28).

places.label: Corinth
places.significance: Paul spent 18 months here; wrote the Thessalonian letters and Romans from here (Acts 18:11).

places.label: Rome
places.significance: Capital of the Roman Empire; Paul preached under house arrest; Peter and Paul martyred here.

places.label: Malta
places.significance: Paul shipwrecked here; 'not one of you will be lost' — serpent, healing of Publius (Acts 27–28).

events.label: Abraham's Call from Ur
events.desc: God calls Abraham from the sophisticated city of Ur with a staggering promise: land, innumerable descendants, and blessing for all nations through his offspring. He goes — not knowing where — the defining act of saving faith.

events.label: Abraham Arrives in Canaan
events.desc: At Shechem, God appears: 'To your offspring I will give this land.' Abraham builds the first altar in Canaan and continues south to Bethel, Egypt during famine, then settles in Hebron — 'a stranger and an exile' holding only the promise.

events.label: The Covenant Cut (Gen 15)
events.desc: God passes through the divided animals in the form of a smoking firepot and flaming torch — ratifying the covenant unilaterally, taking the covenant curse on himself. Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness.

events.label: Isaac — Son of Promise
events.desc: After twenty-five years of waiting, the son of promise is born. Abraham is 100. Sarah laughs. The line of the Messiah begins with an impossible birth to a barren womb and a body 'as good as dead' — a death-and-resurrection pattern that runs through all of Scripture.

events.label: Binding of Isaac
events.desc: God commands Abraham to sacrifice Isaac on Mount Moriah. As the knife is raised, the Angel of the LORD stops him: 'Now I know that you fear God.' A ram is provided — the first substitutionary sacrifice, a 2,000-year preview of Calvary on the same mountain.

events.label: Jacob at Bethel — the Ladder
events.desc: Fleeing Esau, Jacob sleeps at Bethel. He sees a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending — God at the top reconfirming the covenant. Jesus declares himself the true Bethel: 'You will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man' (John 1:51).

events.label: Jacob Wrestles at Peniel
events.desc: Alone the night before facing Esau, Jacob wrestles a divine Man until dawn. His hip is put out of socket; he refuses to let go. 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.' He receives the name Israel — 'one who strives with God' — and walks away limping. The church still walks the same road.

events.label: Joseph Sold into Egypt — Dreams and Providence
events.desc: At seventeen, Joseph is sold by his jealous brothers to Ishmaelite traders at Dothan for twenty pieces of silver. He descends into Egypt as a slave — yet every step of the way, 'the LORD was with Joseph.' His suffering is the means by which God will save a family, a people, and ultimately foreshadow the greater Savior.

events.label: Joseph in Pharaoh's Court — Savior of Nations
events.desc: Thirteen years after his enslavement, Joseph stands before Pharaoh and interprets his dream of seven fat and seven thin cows. God gives wisdom no Egyptian could supply. In a day he moves from prison to the palace: 'There is none so discerning and wise as you.' He becomes Pharaoh's vizier over all Egypt.

events.label: Jacob's Family Enters Egypt
events.desc: Joseph — sold into slavery, falsely imprisoned, supernaturally elevated — invites his family to survive the famine. Seventy souls enter Egypt. 'You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good' (Gen 50:20). The exile of 430 years begins; the exodus is latent in the promise.

events.label: Moses Born — Hidden in the Nile
events.desc: Pharaoh orders every Hebrew son drowned. Moses's mother hides him in a basket among the reeds — the very instrument of death becomes the means of rescue. Pharaoh's own daughter finds and raises him. The deliverer is trained at the court of the oppressor.

events.label: Moses Kills the Egyptian — Flees to Midian
events.desc: Forty years after his birth, Moses sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew slave. He strikes the Egyptian and hides the body in the sand. When Pharaoh hears, he seeks to kill Moses. Moses flees east to the land of Midian — a wandering shepherd, yet the deliverer of a nation, being formed in wilderness obscurity.

events.label: Moses in Midian — Forty Wilderness Years
events.desc: Moses in Midian for forty years — tending the flocks of Jethro his father-in-law in the region east of the Gulf of Aqaba. The prince of Egypt becomes a shepherd. God is at work in the quiet years: humbling, shaping, preparing. Moses at eighty will be a vessel fit for the purpose God planned before his birth.

events.label: The Burning Bush
events.desc: After forty years in Midian, Moses sees a bush burning without being consumed. God speaks: 'I AM WHO I AM. I have seen the affliction of my people in Egypt.' This is the name that defines all of subsequent theology: the self-existent, covenant-keeping God who acts in history.

events.label: The Exodus — Passover Night
events.desc: Ten plagues. The Passover lamb. The death angel passes over every house marked with blood. Israel leaves Egypt after 430 years — 2 million people walking out in a night. 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Cor 5:7). This night is the paradigm of salvation for all time.
events.desc_extended: The ten plagues dismantled Egypt's theology one deity at a time: the Nile god, the sun god, the god of life — each struck down in sequence. The Passover lamb had to be without blemish, slaughtered at twilight, its blood applied to the doorposts with hyssop — the same plant used to offer Jesus sour wine on the cross (John 19:29). The haste of the meal, the unleavened bread, the staff in hand — all of it encoded the urgency of a people not yet home. Paul writes: 'You are not your own; you were bought with a price.' The blood on the doorpost was not a reward for moral achievement; it was pure grace received by faith.

events.label: The Covenant at Sinai
events.desc: Israel camps at Sinai for nearly a year. Ten Commandments. Covenant ratified with blood. The Tabernacle built and filled with the glory cloud. The law is given to a redeemed people — not to earn redemption, but to shape a redeemed community. The gospel of grace always precedes the law.
events.desc_extended: The covenant structure at Sinai follows the pattern of ancient Near Eastern suzerain-vassal treaties: the great king identifies himself, recites his saving acts, then gives the stipulations. God's opening words are not commandments but gospel: 'I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt.' Obedience is the proper response to grace already given, not the means of earning it. The Tabernacle was the climax: a mobile Mount Sinai that God himself designed so his glory could travel with his people. The letter to the Hebrews unpacks every element — the priesthood, the sacrifices, the curtain — as shadows pointing forward to Christ, the better mediator of a better covenant ratified not with the blood of bulls but with his own.

events.label: The Golden Calf & Intercession
events.desc: While Moses receives the law, Israel makes a golden calf at the foot of the mountain of God. Moses intercedes: 'Blot me out of your book.' God relents. Moses's intercession is one of the great types of Christ's priestly prayer — standing between a holy God and a sinful people.

events.label: Moses Dies on Mount Nebo
events.desc: Moses climbs Nebo and God shows him the entire promised land — then he dies at 120, 'his eye undimmed, his vigor unabated.' God himself buries him in an unmarked grave. Joshua is commissioned. A chapter ends; the greater chapter of conquest begins.

events.label: Israel Crosses the Jordan
events.desc: The priests step into the flooding Jordan carrying the ark — and the water stops. Twelve memorial stones are set up at Gilgal. 'What do these stones mean?' The next generation will learn: God parts water for his people. Every conquest begins with a miracle of grace, not human strength.

events.label: Jericho Falls
events.desc: Israel marches around Jericho once daily for six days, seven times on the seventh, then shouts — and the walls fall flat. Only Rahab's household is saved, marked by a scarlet cord in the window. The entire world's greatest military strategy would have failed. Faith in God's word succeeds.

events.label: The Land Divided
events.desc: The whole congregation assembles at Shiloh. The Tabernacle is set up. The land is divided among the twelve tribes by lot — each portion a tangible installment on the promise made to Abraham 700 years earlier. 'Not one word of all the good promises that the LORD had made failed' (Josh 23:14).

events.label: Deborah and Barak — The Kishon Sweeps Them Away
events.desc: Israel has suffered under Jabin's general Sisera and his 900 iron chariots for twenty years. Deborah, prophet and judge, summons Barak and declares the LORD's command: march to Mount Tabor. At her word the LORD routs Sisera's army — his chariots bogged in the Kishon flood. 'The stars fought from heaven; the Kishon River swept them away' (Judg 5:20–21).

events.label: The Cycle of the Judges
events.desc: Abandon → Oppression → Cry out → Deliver → Rest → Repeat. Each cycle ends worse than the last. Deborah, Gideon, Jephthah, Samson. The book's diagnosis is devastating: the human heart without the Spirit of God cannot sustain covenant faithfulness. Israel needs not a better judge but a different king.

events.label: Ruth — 'Where You Go I Will Go'
events.desc: In the days of the judges, a Moabite widow named Ruth clings to her mother-in-law Naomi and to Israel's God. 'Where you go I will go; your people shall be my people, and your God my God.' She gleans in Boaz's field. A kinsman-redeemer takes her as his wife. Ruth, a Gentile outsider, enters the covenant line that leads to King David and to Christ.

events.label: Gideon's 300
events.desc: God reduces Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 — specifically so Israel cannot boast. Three hundred men with torches hidden in clay jars surround the Midianite camp at night. They smash the jars, raise the torches, blow the trumpets. The Midianite army destroys itself in panic. The victory belongs to God alone.

events.label: Samson and Delilah — Gaza and the Temple of Dagon
events.desc: Samson, set apart to God from birth, squanders his calling on the Philistine Delilah. She cuts his hair; 'the LORD has left him.' But in his death Samson kills more Philistines than in his life — 'Let me die with the Philistines' — a preview of death-as-victory that will reach its fullness at Calvary.

events.label: The Ark Captured at Aphek
events.desc: Israel treats the ark as a lucky charm and carries it into battle against the Philistines. Thirty thousand Israelites die. The ark is captured. Eli falls backward and dies. His daughter-in-law names her newborn son Ichabod — 'the glory has departed.' God will not be presumed upon.

events.label: Samuel Anoints David — The Shepherd King
events.desc: God rejects Saul. Samuel goes to Bethlehem to anoint one of Jesse's sons. Seven tall brothers pass before him; God refuses each one. The youngest — a shepherd boy in the field — is sent for. 'The LORD sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the LORD looks on the heart.' The Spirit of the LORD rushes upon David from that day forward.

events.label: Saul Anointed — First King
events.desc: Israel demands a king 'like all the nations.' Samuel warns them of what a king will do — take their sons, daughters, fields, flocks, and freedom. God grants the request as a judgment. Saul, impressive in appearance, is chosen — the answer to wrong desire. David, the man after God's own heart, waits.

events.label: Saul Falls at Gilboa — How the Mighty Have Fallen
events.desc: After years of disobedience, jealousy, and consulting a medium at Endor, Saul meets the Philistines at Mount Gilboa. His sons die; Saul falls on his own sword. David's lament is among the most beautiful in Scripture: 'How the mighty have fallen! Tell it not in Gath.' A kingdom is lost through pride; the faithful king waits in the wings.

events.label: David and Goliath
events.desc: A nine-foot Philistine champion taunts the armies of the living God for forty days. A shepherd boy steps forward: 'You come to me with a sword… but I come to you in the name of the LORD of hosts.' A single stone. Goliath falls. The pattern is set: God's strength through human weakness.

events.label: David Takes Jerusalem
events.desc: David captures the Jebusite stronghold of Zion — belonging to no single tribe, perfect as a capital for all Israel. He brings the ark to Jerusalem with dancing and joy. Then God speaks: 'I will raise up your offspring, and I will establish his throne forever.' The Davidic covenant — the summit of the OT.

events.label: Solomon's Temple
events.desc: Built in seven years from Lebanese cedar and Ophir gold, the Temple is filled at its dedication with the glory cloud — the same cloud that led Israel through the wilderness now rests in a house. Solomon prays: 'Will God indeed dwell on the earth?' The answer is no — until the Word became flesh.
events.desc_extended: The Temple took seven years to build. Ten thousand men a month rotated through Lebanon cutting cedar. Thirty thousand Israelites quarried stone. Hiram of Tyre cast two towering bronze pillars — Jachin ('he establishes') and Boaz ('in him is strength') — flanking the entrance. The Most Holy Place was a perfect cube lined entirely in gold, housing only the ark and the two cherubim with wings spanning the full twenty cubits. At the dedication, the priests could not even stand to minister because the glory cloud was so thick (1 Kgs 8:11). Yet 400 years later Jesus stood in a rebuilt, humbler version of this house and said: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up' — speaking of his body, the true temple God was building from before the foundation of the world.

events.label: Solomon and the Queen of Sheba — Wisdom on Display
events.desc: The Queen of Sheba travels from southern Arabia to test Solomon with hard questions. 'Not even half was told me; your wisdom and prosperity surpass the report that I heard.' She gives him gold, spices, and precious stones beyond anything ever brought before. Jesus points to this scene: 'Something greater than Solomon is here.'

events.label: The Kingdom Divides
events.desc: Rehoboam's folly: he threatens to make the yoke heavier. Ten tribes revolt under Jeroboam. The united kingdom of David and Solomon fractures in a single day. Two golden calves. Two shrines. Two trajectories — both leading to the same exile. The prophets' long ministry of warning begins.

events.label: Elijah on Mount Carmel
events.desc: 450 prophets of Baal call on their god from morning until evening. Nothing. Elijah prays one simple prayer. Fire falls from heaven and consumes the sacrifice, the stones, the wood, the water in the trench. 'The LORD, he is God.' Baal worship's great public failure — yet the apostasy continues.

events.label: Elisha — Naaman Healed and the Syrian Axe-Head Floats
events.desc: Naaman, commander of the Syrian army, comes to Elisha leprous and expecting drama. 'Go, wash in the Jordan seven times.' He nearly refuses — until his servants persuade him. He dips seven times and his flesh is restored 'like the flesh of a little child.' He confesses: 'There is no God in all the earth but in Israel.' The Gentile is healed; the servant Gehazi grasps after reward and contracts the leprosy Naaman lost.

events.label: Elisha and the Chariots of Fire
events.desc: When the king of Aram surrounds Dothan to capture Elisha, his servant panics. Elisha prays: 'LORD, open his eyes.' The servant sees: the mountain is full of horses and chariots of fire around Elisha. The invisible army of God is always greater than the visible armies of the world.

events.label: Jonah at Nineveh — The Whole City Repents
events.desc: Jonah flees God's call across the Mediterranean, spends three days in the belly of a great fish, and arrives at Nineveh — the capital of Israel's greatest enemy. His message is eight words: 'Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown.' The entire city, from king to cattle, repents in sackcloth. Jesus calls Jonah's three days a sign of his own death and resurrection.

events.label: Assyria Rising — Tiglath-Pileser
events.desc: Tiglath-Pileser III rebuilds Assyria into the most efficient military machine the ancient world had seen. He begins deporting conquered peoples to break their national identity — a new and terrifying weapon of empire. Amos and Hosea in the north, Isaiah in the south, all begin warning of judgment.

events.label: Samaria Falls — Israel Destroyed
events.desc: After a three-year siege, Sargon II captures Samaria and deports 27,290 Israelites. The northern kingdom is finished — 200 years, 19 kings, every one evil. 'This happened because the people of Israel sinned against the LORD their God… and feared other gods' (2 Kgs 17:7). Every word Moses warned came true.

events.label: Sennacherib Besieges Jerusalem
events.desc: Sennacherib surrounds Jerusalem. His field commander mocks: 'Do not let Hezekiah deceive you.' Hezekiah spreads the letter before the LORD and prays. Isaiah prophesies. The Angel of the LORD strikes 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in a night. Jerusalem is spared — not for their merit, but for God's name.

events.label: Jeremiah — The Weeping Prophet and the New Covenant
events.desc: From Josiah's reform to Jerusalem's fall — forty years of preaching that Israel largely ignored. Jeremiah wept over the city he was called to condemn. He bought a field at the moment of conquest as a sign of hope. And he proclaimed what no OT prophet had said so clearly: 'I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel… I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts.'

events.label: Josiah's Reformation
events.desc: Eight-year-old Josiah becomes king. At 26 he begins to seek the God of David. The Book of the Law is found in the Temple — apparently lost during generations of apostasy. Josiah reads it, tears his clothes, and leads the greatest covenant renewal in Israel's history. But the heart of the people has not changed.

events.label: Nineveh Falls to Babylon
events.desc: Nahum had prophesied it: 'The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, but the LORD will by no means leave the guilty unpunished.' Nineveh — the city that Jonah's preaching had spared 150 years earlier — falls to the Babylonians and Medes. The rod of God's anger is broken.

events.label: Nebuchadnezzar at Carchemish
events.desc: Nebuchadnezzar crushes Pharaoh Neco at Carchemish — the decisive battle that hands the ancient Near East to Babylon. Within the year, he besieges Jerusalem for the first time. Daniel and his friends are deported. The Babylonian captivity begins its three-wave assault on Judah.

events.label: Ezekiel — Visions by the River Chebar
events.desc: Among the first exiles beside the Chebar River, a young priest named Ezekiel receives a terrifying and glorious vision: a chariot of living creatures, wheels within wheels, and the glory of the LORD. He is overwhelmed seven days. Then God commissions him as a 'watchman' for the house of Israel — to speak whether they hear or refuse to hear.

events.label: Jehoiachin Deported
events.desc: The second deportation: 10,000 of Judah's best — craftsmen, warriors, leaders — are marched to Babylon, including the young prophet Ezekiel. Nebuchadnezzar installs Zedekiah as a puppet king. The city still stands; the Temple still stands. But not for long.

events.label: Jerusalem Falls — Temple Burns
events.desc: Nebuchadnezzar breaks through the walls. The Temple is burned. The bronze pillars, the great bronze sea, all the temple furniture are broken up and carried to Babylon. 'How lonely sits the city that was full of people!' (Lam 1:1). Yet the prophets speak: 'I will make a new covenant' (Jer 31:31).
events.desc_extended: The fall of Jerusalem was the catastrophe Israel's entire history had been warning toward. Moses had said: if you abandon the covenant, you will be scattered among the nations (Deut 28:64). Samuel warned about kings. Every prophet from Elijah forward called Israel to return. The siege lasted eighteen months. When the walls finally broke on the ninth day of the fourth month (a date still mourned in the Jewish calendar as Tisha B'Av), the city was systematically looted, the priests were executed, and the population was marched to Babylon in chains. Yet in the rubble, three voices refused despair. Jeremiah bought a field — a physical act of faith that the land would be redeemed. Ezekiel saw the glory of God depart from the Temple toward the east, then received the vision of dry bones coming to life. Daniel prayed three times a day toward Jerusalem from Babylon, and received visions of a Son of Man who would receive an everlasting kingdom. The exile was not the end of the story.

events.label: Daniel in the Lion's Den
events.desc: Darius decrees that no one may petition any god or man except the king. Daniel prays three times a day as he always has. The lion's den. God sends his angel and shuts the lions' mouths. Darius writes to all peoples: 'He is the living God, enduring forever… he delivers and rescues.' The exile is a mission field.

events.label: Cyrus Conquers Babylon
events.desc: In a single night, Babylon falls. Belshazzar is killed. Cyrus the Great — named by Isaiah 150 years before his birth as God's 'anointed' — issues his decree: the Jews may return and rebuild their Temple. The empire that destroyed Jerusalem is used to restore it. God rules over the rulers of the earth.

events.label: Esther — For Such a Time as This
events.desc: Haman the Agagite plots to exterminate all Jews throughout the Persian Empire. Mordecai urges his adopted daughter Esther, now queen of Persia: 'Who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?' Esther fasts three days, enters the king unbidden at the risk of death, and intercedes. Haman is hanged on the gallows he built for Mordecai. The feast of Purim is established.

events.label: The Second Temple Completed
events.desc: After years of opposition and delay, the second Temple is finished. The elders who had seen Solomon's Temple weep at how modest it is; the young men shout for joy. Haggai promises: 'The latter glory of this house shall be greater than the former.' The greater glory would be a carpenter from Nazareth.

events.label: Nehemiah Rebuilds the Walls
events.desc: Nehemiah hears that Jerusalem's walls lie in ruins; he weeps, fasts, and prays. Artaxerxes gives him letters and timber. The entire wall is rebuilt in 52 days against sustained opposition. Then Ezra reads the law publicly. The people weep. The covenant is renewed. The last chapter before 400 years of silence.

events.label: Malachi — Final Prophet
events.desc: The last word before 400 years of prophetic silence: 'Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' God will send a forerunner — then he himself will come suddenly to his Temple. The stage is set for the great entrance.

events.label: Alexander Takes Palestine
events.desc: Alexander the Great sweeps from Macedonia through Persia to India in thirteen years. Palestine falls without a fight. The ancient Near East is hellenized — Greek language, Greek culture, Greek philosophy. Providentially, the language of the Septuagint and the New Testament will be the lingua franca of the Roman world.

events.label: The Maccabean Revolt
events.desc: Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrates the Temple, sacrifices a pig on the altar, and forbids Torah observance. Mattathias and his sons — the Maccabees — revolt. Against all odds, they recapture Jerusalem and rededicate the Temple. The festival of Hanukkah. Jesus later celebrates it in Jerusalem (John 10:22).

events.label: Pompey Enters the Holy of Holies
events.desc: Rome's general Pompey besieges Jerusalem and enters the Holy of Holies. He looks around — and finds nothing. No cult statue. No treasure. An empty room with a curtain. He leaves the Temple intact, but Israel is now a Roman vassal. The stage is fully set: 'When the fullness of time had come' (Gal 4:4).

events.label: John the Baptist — The Voice in the Wilderness
events.desc: After 400 years of prophetic silence, a voice breaks through from the Judean wilderness. John the Baptist appears — dressed in camel's hair, eating locusts and wild honey — and calls all Israel to repentance. 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.' He baptizes in the Jordan and announces the one who is coming: 'whose sandals I am not worthy to carry.'

events.label: The Incarnation — Word Becomes Flesh
events.desc: The eternal Son of God enters his own creation as a helpless infant in a feeding trough, in an occupied territory, in a backwater village, to a poor family, at night. Angels announce it to shepherds — the lowest of the low. 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us, full of grace and truth' (John 1:14).
events.desc_extended: Every detail of the Incarnation is a deliberate reversal of worldly expectations. The Son through whom all things were made (Col 1:16) was born in a borrowed stable in a town so small that Nathanael asked, 'Can anything good come from Nazareth?' The announcement went not to Caesar, not to the high priest, not to the Sanhedrin — but to shepherds, who ranked among the ritually unclean in first-century Judaism. Magi came from the east — Gentiles, following a star, while Herod's court could cite the correct prophecy (Micah 5:2) and lift no finger to go. The name Immanuel had been promised 700 years earlier to a faithless king (Ahaz) as a sign he would not receive: 'God with us.' Now the sign had arrived. The mystery Paul calls 'great' (1 Tim 3:16) is not just that God became man, but that he became this kind of man — poor, displaced, refugee, obscure — as the pattern he would maintain all the way to Golgotha.

events.label: Jesus Baptized — Ministry Begins
events.desc: John baptizes Jesus in the Jordan. The Father speaks: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.' The Spirit descends as a dove. Then forty days of temptation in the wilderness — Israel's forty years recapitulated in forty days, with one crucial difference: Jesus succeeds where Israel failed.

events.label: Temptation in the Wilderness
events.desc: Immediately after his baptism, the Spirit drives Jesus into the wilderness for forty days. Satan offers bread, spectacle, and world dominion. Jesus answers every temptation with 'It is written.' He is tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin — the second Adam succeeds where the first failed.

events.label: Wedding at Cana — First Sign
events.desc: At a wedding feast in Cana, Jesus turns water into wine — his first sign. Six stone jars for Jewish purification filled to the brim; the best wine saved for last. The sign declares what the whole gospel story tells: the Law's purification rites give way to the abundant grace of the new covenant.

events.label: Sermon on the Mount
events.desc: Jesus sits on a hillside overlooking the Sea of Galilee and delivers the constitution of the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are the poor in spirit. You are the salt of the earth. Love your enemies. The crowds are astonished — 'he taught them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes.' A new Moses has appeared.

events.label: Feeding the Five Thousand
events.desc: Five loaves. Two fish. Five thousand men, plus women and children. Jesus gives thanks, breaks the bread, and distributes it through his disciples until everyone is satisfied. Twelve baskets of fragments remain. The crowd wants to make him king by force; Jesus withdraws to pray. He is the bread of life — but not the kind of king they want.

events.label: Transfiguration
events.desc: Jesus takes Peter, James, and John to a high mountain. His face shines like the sun; his garments become white as light. Moses and Elijah appear — the Law and the Prophets — speaking of 'his departure, which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.' The Father's voice: 'This is my beloved Son… listen to him.' The destination is clear.

events.label: The Galilean Ministry
events.desc: Capernaum. The Sermon on the Mount. Calming the storm. Feeding 5,000. Healing every disease. Twelve disciples. 'The land of Zebulun and Naphtali… the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light' (Matt 4:15–16). The long-promised dawn has broken.

events.label: Triumphal Entry
events.desc: On a colt, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9, Jesus enters Jerusalem to shouts of 'Hosanna to the Son of David!' The city is stirred. The Passover is approaching. The hour has come. Within the week: the upper room, Gethsemane, the arrest, the trials, Golgotha. The King has arrived — not to conquer Rome but to conquer sin and death.

events.label: The Crucifixion and Resurrection
events.desc: The Triumphal Entry. The Temple cleansed. The Last Supper — 'This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many.' Gethsemane. Betrayal. Trial. Golgotha. 'It is finished.' Three days. The stone rolled away. 'He is not here — he has risen.' The hinge of all history. Death itself has been defeated.
events.desc_extended: The crucifixion fulfilled prophecy with a precision that staggers the imagination. Psalm 22, written a thousand years earlier, described the scene in exact detail: garments divided, lots cast, bones out of joint, hands and feet pierced, bystanders mocking with the very words spoken at Golgotha. Isaiah 53 — written 700 years before — described a suffering servant 'wounded for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities,' making many righteous by bearing their iniquity. Jesus was crucified at Passover, outside the city gate (Heb 13:12), as the Passover lambs were being slaughtered in the Temple below. The earth shook. The Temple curtain tore from top to bottom — God tearing open the way to his presence, not from the bottom by human effort but from the top by divine act. Three days later, in the pre-dawn darkness, the tomb was empty. Paul's summary: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins' (1 Cor 15:17). The resurrection is not a metaphor; it is the load-bearing wall of everything Christianity claims.

events.label: Pentecost — The Church Born
events.desc: Fifty days after the resurrection, the Spirit is poured out in Jerusalem with wind and fire. 3,000 baptized in a day. People from every nation under heaven hear in their own tongue 'the mighty works of God.' The new covenant is inaugurated. The church — the new covenant community — is born.

events.label: Philip and the Ethiopian Eunuch — The Gospel Goes to Africa
events.desc: An angel sends Philip south from Samaria to the desert road toward Gaza. He finds the treasurer of Ethiopia reading from Isaiah 53 — 'like a sheep he was led to the slaughter.' 'Do you understand what you are reading?' Philip explains that the servant Isaiah described is Jesus. The eunuch believes and is baptized in the roadside water. The gospel enters Africa.

events.label: Peter and Cornelius — The Gentile Door Opens
events.desc: Peter, while praying at Joppa, sees a vision of unclean animals and hears: 'What God has made clean, do not call common.' Three times. A Roman centurion named Cornelius has already received an angelic vision pointing him to Peter. Peter arrives, hears their story, and declares: 'God shows no partiality.' The Holy Spirit falls on the Gentiles before baptism. The door that was closed is opened.

events.label: Paul's Conversion on the Damascus Road
events.desc: Saul of Tarsus — Hebrew of Hebrews, Pharisee, persecutor of the church — is struck down by a blinding light. 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?' The risen Jesus meets him on the road. The church's greatest enemy becomes its greatest missionary. 'He is a chosen instrument of mine' (Acts 9:15).

events.label: Antioch — Paul and Barnabas Sent Out
events.desc: The Holy Spirit speaks through the prophets at Antioch: 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.' The church fasts, prays, and lays hands on them — the first deliberate missionary sending in church history. Antioch, where believers were first called 'Christians,' becomes the launching pad of the Gentile mission.

events.label: Paul's First Missionary Journey
events.desc: From Antioch, Paul and Barnabas sail to Cyprus and then into Galatia — planting churches in Iconium, Lystra, Derbe. Paul is stoned at Lystra and left for dead. He gets up and continues. The pattern of the mission: suffering and resurrection, death and fruitfulness, the cross preceding the crown.

events.label: Cyprus — Salamis and Paphos
events.desc: Paul and Barnabas preach through the whole island of Cyprus from Salamis to Paphos. At Paphos, the proconsul Sergius Paulus seeks to hear the word of God; the sorcerer Elymas opposes them and is struck blind. Sergius Paulus believes, astonished at the teaching of the Lord.

events.label: Pisidian Antioch — Synagogue Sermon
events.desc: In the synagogue at Pisidian Antioch, Paul preaches the gospel through Israel's history from Egypt to the resurrection of Christ. 'Through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you.' The Gentiles beg him to continue; the next Sabbath nearly the whole city gathers. The Jews stir up persecution. 'We are turning to the Gentiles.'

events.label: Lystra — Healing, Worship, Stoning
events.desc: At Lystra Paul heals a man lame from birth. The crowd erupts: 'The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!' Paul tears his garments: 'We are men of like nature with you!' Jews from Antioch and Iconium arrive; they stone Paul and drag him out of the city, supposing him dead. He rises and goes back in.

events.label: Macedonia — Gospel Enters Europe
events.desc: 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' At Philippi, Lydia believes — the first European convert. Paul and Silas are imprisoned; they sing hymns at midnight; an earthquake opens the doors; the jailer asks: 'What must I do to be saved?' The gospel has crossed from Asia to Europe.

events.label: Troas — The Macedonian Call
events.desc: Blocked by the Holy Spirit from Asia and Bithynia, Paul arrives at Troas. That night, a vision: a man of Macedonia pleading, 'Come over to Macedonia and help us.' Immediately they seek to go — concluding that God has called them to preach the gospel to Europe. The continent that will carry the gospel to the world is about to hear it.

events.label: Philippi — Lydia and the Jailer
events.desc: At Philippi, Lydia — a dealer in purple — opens her heart. Paul casts out a spirit from a slave girl; he and Silas are beaten and imprisoned. At midnight they sing hymns; an earthquake opens the doors; the jailer asks 'What must I do to be saved?' 'Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ.' The first European church is planted.

events.label: Athens — The Areopagus
events.desc: Paul's spirit is provoked by the idols filling Athens. In the Areopagus he declares the unknown God: the one who made the world, who gives life and breath, in whom we live and move and have our being. He calls for repentance because God has appointed a day of judgment — proved by raising Jesus from the dead. Some mock; some believe.

events.label: Corinth — Eighteen Months
events.desc: In Corinth, Paul meets Priscilla and Aquila and works as a tentmaker. He teaches in the synagogue every Sabbath. The Lord appears in a vision: 'Do not be afraid; keep on speaking, for I am with you.' Paul stays eighteen months. From Corinth he writes the Thessalonian letters; later Romans is written from here.

events.label: Paul in Rome — Preaching Without Hindrance
events.desc: Shipwrecked at Malta, Paul finally reaches Rome under house arrest. For two years he receives all who come — 'proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance' (Acts 28:31). The last word of Acts. The mission continues.

events.label: Three Years at Ephesus
events.desc: Paul's longest stay: three years in Ephesus, capital of the province of Asia. 'All who lived in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks' (Acts 19:10). Extraordinary miracles. A riot in the theater. From here, the gospel radiates to Smyrna, Pergamum, Laodicea — the seven churches of Revelation.

events.label: Miletus — Farewell to the Elders
events.desc: Paul summons the Ephesian elders to Miletus — his last meeting with them. 'I do not account my life of any value nor as precious to myself, if only I may finish my course.' He warns of wolves and commits them to God and the word of his grace. They weep and embrace him, sorrowful that they will see his face no more.

events.label: Jerusalem and the Temple Destroyed
events.desc: Titus and the Roman legions destroy Jerusalem and burn the Temple — exactly as Jesus had prophesied in AD 30 (Matt 24:2). Over one million die. The sacrificial system ends permanently. But the true Temple — the body of Christ — has already been raised. The church scatters and the gospel accelerates.

events.label: Revelation — Christ's Final Word
events.desc: John is exiled on Patmos. The risen Christ appears in blazing glory and dictates seven letters to seven churches — then the full vision of history's end: the Lamb on the throne, seals opened, trumpets sounded, the great harlot fallen, the beast defeated, and the New Jerusalem descending like a bride. 'Come, Lord Jesus.'
